original_reasoning: Arabic worst lameness gives bodily crookedness or defective gait, and Greek is defective speech; this is a clear metaphorical chain from physical distortion to linguistic error.
semantic_journey: Arabic القزل (al-qazal) = worst form of lameness, severe limping gait defect. Greek σολοικίζω = to speak incorrectly, make a grammatical/linguistic error, speak 'like a Soloi person' (from Soloi, a city famous for Greek dialect errors). The semantic path: physical defect in gait → metaphorical defect in speech. Arabic bodily crookedness/lameness → Greek linguistic crookedness/error is a 1-step metaphorical extension (physical defect → linguistic defect). This is a documented semantic drift pattern.
key_insight: Arabic severe lameness (qazal) maps metaphorically to Greek linguistic error (soloikismos) — both encode defective/crooked performance.

original_reasoning: Arabic qasr = 'palace, castle, court' (derived via Aramaic from Latin castrum, same root as Caesar); Greek = 'Caesar'. Plausible Mediterranean etymological connection via the Roman loanword.
semantic_journey: Arabic قصر (qasr) = palace, castle, court; also: to shorten/restrict. The original reasoning acknowledges that Arabic قصر is itself derived from Latin castrum via Aramaic — it is a Latin loanword into Arabic. Greek Καίσαρι = Caesar (dative of Καῖσαρ). Both Arabic qasr and Greek Caesar ultimately derive from Latin (castrum and Caesar are different Latin words, but both are Latin-origin). This is not an Arabic-Greek cognate — both words come from Latin. Shared loanword from Latin.
key_insight: Arabic qasr (palace) derives from Latin castrum; Greek Kaisar is Latin Caesar — both are Latin-origin terms, not an Arabic-Greek cognate.

original_reasoning: نبت=to grow/sprout; Ναβαταῖος=Nabataean (people name from Semitic root). Strong Semitic cognate via ethnonym
semantic_journey: Arabic نبت (nabata) = to grow, sprout, germinate. Greek Ναβαταῖος = Nabataean, the ethnonym of the Nabataean people (the trading civilization centered in Petra). The Nabataean name (Semitic nbṭ) is a proper ethnic/place name. The Arabic root نبت may share the same Semitic root as the ethnic name, but this is an ethnonym — a proper name of a people. Excluded per proper-name rule.
key_insight: Ναβαταῖος is the proper ethnic name for the Nabataean people; comparing it to Arabic grow/sprout treats a proper name as a common noun.

original_reasoning: Arabic استرخى بطنه centers on slackening or giving way, closely matching Greek gave way or retreated.
semantic_journey: Arabic خظظ (khazza) / استرخى بطنه = the belly slackened/became loose; giving way/slackening of the belly. Greek χάζετο = he drew back, gave way, retreated (imperfect of χάζομαι). Both encode giving way/loosening. Arabic belly slackening/giving way → Greek physical retreating/giving way is a 1-step drift from bodily slackening to general giving way/retreat — the same yielding concept at body → action level.
key_insight: Arabic belly-slackening/yielding (khazz) and Greek retreating/giving-way (khazetai) share the core of yielding/slackening.

original_reasoning: Arabic خص has the core of assigning specially or making something one's own, close to Greek have/hold.
semantic_journey: Arabic خصص (khassasa) = to specify, assign specially, make particular to one person; to appropriate/reserve exclusively. Greek σχήσουσα = she who will have/hold (future participle of ἔχω 'to have/hold'). Both encode the concept of holding/possessing something specifically one's own. Arabic exclusive-assignment → Greek having/holding is a 1-step drift: to assign exclusively to oneself is to hold it.
key_insight: Arabic exclusive-assignment (khasas) and Greek having/holding (ekhein) share the concept of something being specifically one's own.

original_reasoning: Arabic folding and Greek helix both preserve a turning, bending, or coiling conceptual core.
semantic_journey: Arabic الكون (al-kawwana/al-kawn) variant form = the folding; Arabic كون from كوى = to fold, wind, coil. Greek ἑλίκοιν = of spirals/coils. Both encode coiling/winding movement. Arabic folding/winding → Greek spiral/coil is a direct 1-step match of the geometric winding concept.
key_insight: Arabic folding/winding (kawn) and Greek spiral (helix) share the coiling geometric concept.

original_reasoning: birdhaun=packhorse/mule vs bourdon=mule; same meaning, Arabic borrowed from Greek (via Byzantine)
semantic_journey: Arabic برذون (birdhaun) = a packhorse or mule, heavy working horse. Greek βουρδών = mule, packhorse. The original reasoning acknowledges that 'Arabic borrowed from Greek (via Byzantine).' This is the REVERSE direction — Arabic borrowed from Greek βουρδών (itself possibly from Gaulish or Latin burdo). This is not an Arabic-to-Greek cognate; it is Greek-to-Arabic. The direction of borrowing is inverted. False positive due to wrong direction.
key_insight: Arabic birdhaun was borrowed FROM Greek bourdon (mule); the direction is Greek→Arabic, not Arabic→Greek.

original_reasoning: Arabic gloss centers on continued blood flow beyond its normal days; Greek gloss centers on copious pouring. The chain through abundant flowing is clear.
semantic_journey: Arabic حيض (haydh) = menstrual blood flow; continued flowing of blood beyond normal period. Greek χυδαῖος = poured out copiously, flowing freely, also: common/vulgar (from χέω 'to pour'). Both encode the concept of copious flowing/pouring. Arabic continued copious blood flow → Greek copious outpouring/flowing is a direct 1-step match: both describe excessive flowing of liquid beyond its proper bounds.
key_insight: Arabic copious blood flow (haydh) and Greek copious outpouring (khudaios) share the concept of liquid flowing beyond normal bounds.

original_reasoning: Arabic أفازه = caused him to attain, and Greek = you have taken/received. This is a clear attainment/acquisition chain.
semantic_journey: Arabic الفوز (al-fawz) = success, attainment, winning; أفازه = he caused him to attain/gain/win. Greek εἰλήφατε = you have taken/received (perfect of λαμβάνω 'to take, receive, obtain'). Both encode the concept of obtaining/receiving/gaining something. Arabic causing-to-attain/win → Greek you-have-obtained is a direct 1-step match: attainment = receiving/taking possession of the goal.
key_insight: Arabic attainment/winning (fawz) and Greek having-taken/received (eilephate) share the core concept of obtaining something successfully.

original_reasoning: Arabic qintar = unit of weight (100 raṭl); Greek kentenarion = 100 librae, unit of weight. Both are Latin-derived centenarium but Arabic qintar and Greek kentenarion share the same Mediterranean measurement-term stratum.
semantic_journey: Arabic قنطار (qintar) = a unit of weight (100 ratls); itself from Latin centenarium via Aramaic. Greek κεντηνάριον = 100 librae, also from Latin centenarium. Both Arabic qintar and Greek kentenarion are loanwords from the SAME Latin source word (centenarium). This is a shared loanword from Latin, not an Arabic-to-Greek cognate.
key_insight: Both Arabic qintar and Greek kentenarion derive from Latin centenarium; this is Latin transmitted into two languages separately, not Arabic-to-Greek.

original_reasoning: إثم = sin, guilt, moral transgression; تحرّج عنه وكفّ = abstained from it scrupulously. Θέμις = divine law, right order, what is divinely ordained. These are two sides of the SAME moral-legal concept: إثم is the violation of Θέμις. Sin exists only relative to divine law. The phonetic match (th-m) reinforces this. This is a strong cognate candidate — the concept split into 'the law' vs 'breaking the law'.
semantic_journey: Arabic اثم (ithm) = sin, moral transgression, guilt — what is morally forbidden. Greek Θέμις = divine law, right order, sacred custom — what is divinely ordained. These are two sides of the same moral-legal concept: ithm is the VIOLATION, Themis is the LAW being violated. Arabic moral-transgression → Greek divine-law is a negation-cognate relationship: the Arabic word defines the wrong act, the Greek word defines the right order that was broken.
key_insight: Arabic sin/transgression (ithm) and Greek divine law (Themis) are the two sides of the same moral-legal concept: violation vs. the law violated.

original_reasoning: Arabic خضر strongly evokes green or unripe freshness, matching Greek unripe grain well.
semantic_journey: Arabic خضر (khudr/akhdar) = green, verdant, fresh, unripe (specifically the fresh green state before ripening). Greek χῖδρον = unripe grain, parched unripe barley grains. Both encode the unripe/fresh green state of grain. Arabic green-unripe → Greek unripe-grain is a direct 1-step specialization: Arabic covers the general fresh/green state, Greek names the specific unripe grain product.
key_insight: Arabic green/unripe (khudr) directly matches Greek unripe grain (khidron) — the freshness/unripeness concept preserved in grain terminology.

original_reasoning: Eaten by people and loved by sheep fits a milk-food pastoral field.
semantic_journey: Arabic القشنيزه (al-qushnaiza) = coriander (Coriandrum sativum), a culinary herb. Greek γάλαξιν = related to γάλα (milk); possibly referring to a milky plant or the galaxy. The reasoning 'eaten by people and loved by sheep' connects only at the broadest agricultural level. Coriander and milk/milky-plants are entirely different botanical categories. No meaningful semantic path.
key_insight: Coriander and Greek milk-related term share only the loosest pastoral association; completely different botanical and semantic categories.

original_reasoning: Arabic crafted mosaic-like work and Greek 'plait, braid' share a concrete made-pattern concept.
semantic_journey: Arabic سرنج (saranj) = a form of crafted mosaic or inlaid work; intricate patterned material. Greek σαργάνη = plaited/braided rope, a woven cord or basket made by plaiting. Both encode intricate material patterning through repeated inter-weaving. Arabic inlaid-pattern craft → Greek plaited/braided work is a 1-step parallel: both describe structured interlacing to create a composite material.
key_insight: Arabic inlaid mosaic-work (saranj) and Greek plaited rope/basket (sargane) share the conceptual core of structured interlacing.

original_reasoning: Arabic q-ʿ-s denotes bent or arched shape, and Greek helix is twisted or spiral; the shape concept persists clearly.
semantic_journey: Arabic القعس (al-qas) = bent backward, arched shape (specifically the backward arch of the spine or a bow). Greek ἕλιξ = spiral, coil, anything curved or twisted. Both encode a specific curved/arched geometric form. Arabic backward-arch → Greek spiral/coil is the same 1-step generalization pattern seen in GRC_0159 and GRC_0182: Arabic names a specific arc, Greek generalizes to any coil/curve.
key_insight: Arabic backward-arch form (qaas) and Greek spiral/coil (helix) share the geometric core of curved form.

original_reasoning: Arabic warmth directly supports the result of softening; Greek 'soften, knead' is a clear semantic continuation.
semantic_journey: Arabic دفيء (dafi) = warmth, being warm, gentle warmth. Greek δέφω = to soften by warmth, to knead, to work leather or bread dough by warming. Both encode warmth-based softening. Arabic warmth → Greek soften-by-warmth is a direct 1-step connection: dafi is the heat, depho is what heat does to material (softens it).
key_insight: Arabic warmth (dafi) and Greek soften-by-warmth/knead (depho) share the core of gentle heat acting on material.

original_reasoning: Smoke naturally spreads and diffuses; that gives a clear concrete chain to Greek pouring out or scattering.
semantic_journey: Arabic دخن (dukhan) = smoke; to smoke, emit smoke. Greek διαχύνω = to pour out through, diffuse, spread widely. Both encode the diffusing/spreading action of something that moves through space. Arabic smoke spreading/diffusing → Greek diffusing/spreading outward is a 1-step parallel: smoke is the canonical example of something that pours and diffuses through the air.
key_insight: Arabic smoke (dukhan) spreading through air maps directly to Greek diffusing/pouring-through (diakhy) — smoke as the model of diffusion.

original_reasoning: اللَّقْلاق and babbler/croaker share a direct noisy vocal sense.
semantic_journey: Arabic اللق (al-laqq/laqlaqa) = to make a loud repetitive sound; لقلاق = stork, named for its bill-clattering noise. Greek λάλαξ = a babbler, one who chatters or croaks. Both are onomatopoeic/phonosemantic words for noisy, repetitive vocalization. Arabic noisy-clattering/chattering sound → Greek babbler/croaker is a direct 1-step match in the sound-production semantic field.
key_insight: Arabic laqlaqa (noisy chattering, stork) and Greek lalax (babbler) share the onomatopoeic concept of repetitive noisy vocalization.

original_reasoning: Arabic stingy descriptor and Greek very harsh/difficult meet in a clear severity concept.
semantic_journey: Arabic حلتب (hultub) = stingy, miserly, one who withholds. Greek χαλεπώτατος = most difficult, harshest, most severe (superlative of χαλεπός). Stinginess and harshness/difficulty are both negative qualities but they are categorically different: stinginess is about withholding goods, harshness is about severity of conditions or personality. The two concepts do not share a 1-2 step semantic path.
key_insight: Arabic stinginess (withholding) and Greek harshness/difficulty are distinct negative qualities with no clean semantic bridge.

original_reasoning: Arabic filling by flood or a water-filled place aligns well with the Greek river gloss.
semantic_journey: Arabic سجر (sajara) = to fill with water (a river flooded/filled), to inflame (a fire), to kindle. Greek Σάγρα = the Sagra River in Calabria, a proper toponym. While the Arabic root has water-filling senses, Σάγρα is a proper place name. Even if the toponym derived from a Semitic root meaning filled-with-water, the Greek form is a proper name and should be excluded. Additionally, the phonetic match is marginal.
key_insight: Sagra is a Greek river toponym; even if Semitic-influenced, comparing to Arabic sajara treats a proper name as a common noun.

original_reasoning: Arabic zawj = pair/couple/yoking together (married couple, pair). Greek Ζεῦγμα = 'joining/yoking' (from ζεύγνυμι 'to yoke'), originally a bridge/place of joining the Euphrates; the toponym's etymology is literally 'yoking'. Arabic zawj and Greek zeugos/zeugma share the conceptual core 'pair/yoke' — this is a well-known Semitic-IE yoking etymon chain (cf. Latin iugum, Sanskrit yuga). The place-name is derived from the very concept matching the Arabic.
semantic_journey: Arabic زوج (zawj) = a pair, couple; to be paired/married; to yoke together. Greek Ζεῦγμα = a joining, a yoke; name of the city at the Euphrates crossing ('the bridge/joining'). The shared concept is yoking/joining two things together. Arabic paired-couple/yoking → Greek joining/bridge is a direct 1-step match: both encode the joining of two separate things. Note: Zeugma as a place name refers directly to a bridge/joining, not a person's name — it is a common-noun toponym, which makes this a material-culture transmission of the yoke/join concept.
key_insight: Arabic zawj (pairing/yoking) and Greek Zeugma (joining/yoke) share the Semitic concept of joining two elements — transmitted in the bridge/yoking toponym.

original_reasoning: Something that befalls or closes in maps well to an afflicted bodily condition.
semantic_journey: Arabic حاق (haqqa) = to befall, descend upon, be realized (what was decreed comes to pass). Greek καχέκτης = one in poor bodily condition, someone with bad constitution (κακός 'bad' + ἕξις 'condition'). The connection requires: what befalls → bad condition that befalls someone → person suffering a bad condition — that is 3+ steps. Greek kakektes has transparent internal etymology (bad + holding/condition) with no Semitic required.
key_insight: Arabic befalling/descending and Greek poor-bodily-condition are too distant; the Greek compound has transparent internal etymology.

original_reasoning: Arabic settling or becoming fixed aligns well with Greek 'had held' through the shared notion of firm holding or established state.
semantic_journey: Arabic ساخ (sakha) = to settle, sink, become fixed in place (sand, ground absorbing). Greek ἐσχήκει = he had held/possessed (perfect of ἔχω). The connection settling/sinking-into-place → having/holding requires: Arabic settling-fixation → established state → holding. The sinking-into-earth sense of sakha and the possession sense of ekhein are semantically distant enough that the path requires 3 steps. Same issue as GRC_0134.
key_insight: Arabic sinking/settling into ground and Greek having/holding are too conceptually distant for a 2-step cognate path.

original_reasoning: Arabic 'invoked against his enemy' strongly meets Greek destruction/perishing through the curse-to-ruin chain.
semantic_journey: Arabic القنوت (al-qunut) = a specific Islamic prayer form; also: devotion, submission, prolonged standing in prayer. Also: to curse enemies (qunut an-nawazil). Greek ὀλέκοντο = they were being destroyed/perishing (imperfect of ὄλλυμι). The connection: cursing enemies → their destruction. While a curse may lead to destruction, the Arabic word primarily means prayerful devotion/submission; the destruction-curse sense is secondary. The chain prayerful-devotion → curse-invocation → enemy-destruction is 3+ steps.
key_insight: Arabic devotional prayer/submission and Greek perishing/destruction are too distant even via the curse-invocation intermediate.

original_reasoning: Balsam juice; see above.
semantic_journey: Arabic بلصم (balsam/bilsam) = balsam resin, the precious aromatic substance. Greek ὀποβάλσαμον = juice of balsam (ὀπός 'juice/sap' + βάλσαμον 'balsam'). The root b-l-s-m is a Semitic word for the balsam plant and its resin that traveled the ancient trade routes. Arabic balsam resin → Greek balsam juice is a direct material culture preservation. The Semitic root balsam is the origin of the Greek word.
key_insight: Arabic balsam resin (bilsam) is the Semitic origin of Greek balsamon — the precious aromatic resin traveled with its Semitic name.

original_reasoning: Arabic ghazu = raid/invade; Greek Gaza from Semitic 'azza (strong). Both are Semitic substrate, shared place-name etymology plausible.
semantic_journey: Arabic غزو (ghazw) = to raid, invade, wage war. Greek Γάζα = Gaza, the city name. While the original reasoning notes that Greek Gaza derives from Semitic 'azza (strong), the Arabic root غزو (raid) is a DIFFERENT Semitic root. Gaza's Semitic etymology is from 'zz (strength), not ghzw (raid). These are distinct roots; the phonetic similarity is coincidental. Furthermore, Γάζα is a proper toponym. Excluded.
key_insight: Γάζα is a Semitic toponym from root 'zz (strong), not from Arabic ghazw (raid) — different roots, and it is a proper name.

original_reasoning: Arabic انثنى matches the Greek notion of something twisted or winding.
semantic_journey: Arabic القعص (al-qas) = twisted, bent; also انثنى = bent back. Greek ἕλιξ = spiral, coil, any curved/wound form. Arabic bending/twisting → Greek spiral/coil is the same arc-to-coil pattern seen in GRC_0159, GRC_0182, GRC_0197. The curved geometry concept is preserved across this family of Arabic roots.
key_insight: Arabic twisted/bent (qaas) and Greek spiral/coil (helix) share the geometric core of curving form.

original_reasoning: خوض is entering or wading into something, and χυδαῖος is poured forth abundantly; both meet strongly in the image of fluid spreading and immersion.
semantic_journey: Arabic خوض (khawd) = to wade into, plunge through, enter a fluid medium; to be immersed in water. Greek χυδαῖος = poured out, flowing abundantly, copious (from χέω 'to pour'). Both encode the concept of liquid spreading/moving through space in abundance. Arabic wading-into/immersing-in-fluid → Greek copious-flowing-outward is a 1-step parallel: both capture the image of liquid movement without restraint.
key_insight: Arabic wading/immersing in fluid (khawd) and Greek copious outpouring (khudaios) share the image of unrestrained liquid movement.

original_reasoning: Βυσναῖοι denotes the people of Bysna/Beisan, which aligns well with the Arabic gloss naming Beisan as a place in Syria.
semantic_journey: Arabic بسن (Baysan) = Beisan, the Arabic name for the ancient city Beth-Shean in the Jordan Valley. Greek Βυσναῖοι = the people of Bysna/Beisan. Both refer to the same Semitic settlement Beit Shean/Baysan. This is a shared proper toponym/ethnonym transmitted via the Semitic name of the city. Excluded: proper name.
key_insight: Both forms name the same city (Beisan/Beth-Shean) and its inhabitants; this is a shared Semitic toponym, not a cognate.

original_reasoning: Arabic خيشوم and nasal openings align well with Greek chasms as openings; concrete aperture is the common core.
semantic_journey: Arabic خشم (khashm) = nose, snout; the nasal passage/opening; also: to break the nose. Greek χάσματα = chasms, gaping openings. Both encode an aperture/opening in a surface. Arabic nasal-opening/nostril → Greek gaping-opening is a 1-step generalization: the nostril is a specific opening in the face; chasm is a general gaping opening in the earth. The core is the same: a void opening through a solid surface.
key_insight: Arabic nostril/nasal-opening (khashm) generalizes to Greek gaping chasm (khasma) — both encode an opening through a solid surface.

original_reasoning: ἀλλάσσω means to change or alter, and the Arabic gloss centers on rows moving from their places and people going off scattered; both share the core of positional change.
semantic_journey: Arabic الصعصع (al-saasa) = rows moving from their places; people scattering from their positions. Greek ἀλλάσσω = to change, alter, exchange, substitute. The connection scattered-movement → change is too broad — any kind of movement could be called change. The Arabic specifically encodes dispersal/scattering, while Greek ἀλλάσσω encodes exchange/alteration. These are different types of change. No clean 2-step path.
key_insight: Arabic scattered dispersal and Greek exchange/alteration are different types of change; no specific semantic path connects them.

original_reasoning: Arabic includes a plant gloss and Greek names a plant; species differ, but plant-to-plant is a clear masadiq chain.
semantic_journey: Arabic الغاغ (al-ghagh) = a plant name (perhaps a shrub or weed). Greek γάλαγγα = galangal (Alpinia galanga), the Asian spice. Both are plant names, but different plants in different botanical families with different properties. Galangal is a Southeast Asian spice that entered Mediterranean trade; Arabic الغاغ names an unrelated local plant. Plant-category matching is insufficient.
key_insight: Two unrelated plant names sharing only the botanical category is not sufficient for a cognate claim.

original_reasoning: Kalaf - skin discoloration/covering vs 'sheath, case, covering' - strong covering/envelope match
semantic_journey: Arabic كلف (kalaf) = skin discoloration, blemish on the skin; the changed/marked surface of skin; also: anything covering the surface. Greek κέλυφος = shell, husk, outer covering, case (the outer layer that encloses something). Both encode the concept of an outer surface layer. Arabic skin-discoloration/surface-marking → Greek outer-shell/husk/covering is a 1-step parallel: both refer to the outermost covering layer of something.
key_insight: Arabic skin surface/blemish (kalaf) and Greek shell/outer-covering (kelyphos) share the concept of an outer surface layer.

original_reasoning: Arabic belly and unending eating connect clearly to Greek cow dung through the action-to-result chain of consumption and excretion.
semantic_journey: Arabic البطن (al-batn) = belly, abdomen; interior. Greek βόλβιτον = cow dung. The reasoning: belly → eating → excretion → dung. This is a 3+ step chain (belly → food intake → digestion → waste output → dung). Each step loses meaning from the previous. Belly is not semantically close enough to dung — they are in the same digestive system but at opposite ends. This is false metaphorical overreach.
key_insight: Arabic belly and Greek cow dung are too many steps apart in the digestive chain; this is not a valid 2-step semantic path.

original_reasoning: epiboētheō=come to aid; b3th=send, dispatch (especially army). Strong semantic+phonetic chain b-3-th/b-o-ē-th.
semantic_journey: Arabic بعث (bath) = to send, dispatch, awaken, rouse; specifically to send a military force or messenger. Greek ἐπιβοηθέω = to come to aid, send assistance, dispatch help (ἐπί 'to/upon' + βοηθέω 'to aid'). Both encode sending/dispatching toward someone in need. Arabic dispatching-forces/messengers → Greek sending-aid is a direct 1-step match: both describe the act of sending someone/something to a destination for a purpose.
key_insight: Arabic sending/dispatching (bath) and Greek sending-aid (epiboetheo) share the core concept of dispatching someone toward a goal.

original_reasoning: Arabic 'tight skin-container' strongly meets Greek 'bottle gourd' by the container/vessel function.
semantic_journey: Arabic القنوت (al-qunut) = devotion, submissive standing in prayer; the qunut supplication. Greek κολοκύνθη = bottle gourd. The original reasoning ('tight skin-container → bottle gourd') uses a completely different reading of القنوت than the standard meaning. The primary meaning is prayerful devotion/submission. Even if an alternate reading of qanawat (pipes/tubes) were used, bottle gourd is not a tube/container in the relevant sense. No path.
key_insight: Arabic prayer-devotion (qunut) and Greek bottle gourd have no semantic relationship under any reading of the Arabic.

original_reasoning: Arabic 'show/reveal' aligns well with Greek 'interpretations/acceptations' as revealed or received meanings.
semantic_journey: Arabic دقه (daqqa) = to show, demonstrate, reveal; also to be precise. Greek ἐκδοχαί = interpretations, acceptations, meanings taken from a text (from ἐκδέχομαι 'to receive from, interpret'). The connection showing/revealing → received-interpretations requires: reveal → what is revealed → how it is received → interpretation — that is 3+ steps. Furthermore, Greek ἐκδοχαί is specifically about receiving/extracting meaning from a text, not about showing or revealing.
key_insight: Arabic showing/revealing and Greek text-interpretations require too many steps; Greek ekdokhai derives from 'receiving from' not 'showing.'

original_reasoning: Clear chain through speech: Arabic has 'much talking'; Greek is 'you were spoken/said'.
semantic_journey: Arabic الكتيت (al-katit) = much talking, chattering, low murmuring. Greek ἐλέχθητε = you were spoken/said (aorist passive of λέγω). Same issue as GRC_0167: Arabic disordered/copious speech vs. neutral Greek speaking verb. The Greek form is simply a past passive of a common speaking verb with no connotation of volume or disorder. No specific semantic path.
key_insight: Arabic chattering/much-talking compared to a neutral Greek speaking verb strips the distinctive Arabic meaning; no valid path.

original_reasoning: Arabic خط can denote a tract or place on the ground, matching Greek earth as the concrete spatial base.
semantic_journey: Arabic خطط (khatta) = to draw lines, mark out a tract of land; a tribal territory defined by lines/marks. Greek χθονί = of the earth (dative of χθών 'earth, ground'). The reasoning: marked-territory → ground → earth. Arabic خط means a line or tract, not earth itself. The connection requires: drawn-line/territory → ground under it → earth in general — that is 3 steps of abstraction. χθών is a deep Greek word for earth/ground with its own distinct etymology.
key_insight: Arabic territory/line-marking and Greek earth/ground are too abstract a connection; χθών has its own Greek etymology.

original_reasoning: low/downwardness naturally yields stumble or false step; this is a clear semantic chain.
semantic_journey: Arabic سفل (safala) = to be low, descend to a lower position; سافل = low, base. Greek σφάλμα = a stumble, false step, error, failure (from σφάλλω 'to trip, make fall'). Both encode downward-falling failure. Arabic being-low/sinking-down → Greek stumbling/falling is a direct 1-step path: sinking-low is what happens when one stumbles/falls. The physical downward motion is preserved.
key_insight: Arabic sinking-low (safl) and Greek stumbling/false-step (sphalma) share the concept of downward falling/failure.

original_reasoning: Arabic مسلاخ refers to sloughed skin or stripped outer layer; Greek names a rough-skinned cartilaginous fish, a clear concrete chain.
semantic_journey: Arabic سلخ (salakha) = to strip, flay, skin; to peel off the outer layer. Arabic مسلاخ = the sloughed skin, the shed outer layer. Greek σέλαχος = a cartilaginous fish (shark or ray), named for its rough skin. The semantic path: Arabic describes the stripping/shedding of skin → the rough outer skin that can be stripped → a fish named for its characteristic rough-skin surface. This is a 2-step material culture path: Arabic skin-stripping concept → the distinctive rough skin property → Greek fish named for that property.
key_insight: Arabic skin-stripping (salakh) connects to Greek rough-skinned fish (selakhos) through the distinctive skin property that defines the fish.

original_reasoning: Arabic 'wrinkled/folded belly' matches Greek 'helix/spiral' well through the shared core of folds and coils.
semantic_journey: Arabic العكنه (al-ukna) = the fold of skin on the belly; wrinkled flesh that folds over. Greek ἑλίκοιν = of spirals, coils (genitive plural of ἕλιξ). Both encode folding/coiling. Arabic belly-fold/wrinkle → Greek spiral/coil is a 1-step generalization: both describe curved material that loops back on itself. The fold of skin is a specific form of the general coiling concept.
key_insight: Arabic belly fold/wrinkle (ukna) and Greek spiral/coil (helix) share the geometric concept of curved material folding back.

original_reasoning: Arabic ghamal = accumulated (trees/clouds); Greek Gamala = Gamla in Golan Heights, Semitic place name. Shared Semitic substrate for place name plausible.
semantic_journey: Arabic غمل (ghamal) = to gather, accumulate (clouds, trees). Greek Γάμαλα = Gamla, an ancient fortified city in the Golan Heights. The original reasoning acknowledges this is a Semitic place name. The actual Semitic etymology of Gamla is from gml (camel) — the city was on a camel-shaped ridge. Arabic غمل (accumulate) is a different root from gml (camel). Excluded: proper toponym; also different root.
key_insight: Gamla is a proper Semitic toponym (from gml 'camel shape'); Arabic ghamal (accumulate) is a different root entirely.

original_reasoning: Greek 'well-aimed' links clearly to Arabic slaughter/victim through striking the target and the result.
semantic_journey: Arabic سطح (sath) = flat surface, slaughter/sacrifice. Greek εὐστόχοις = well-aimed (dative plural of εὔστοχος 'good-aiming'). The reasoning: Arabic slaughter/striking → Greek well-aimed strike. But Arabic سطح primarily means flat surface; the slaughter sense is secondary. Even using the slaughter sense, the chain is: slaughtering → striking → aiming well to strike — the target concept of εὔστοχος (hitting a mark) is semantically different from Arabic flat surface or even slaughter. No clean path.
key_insight: Arabic flat surface / slaughter and Greek well-aimed do not share a direct semantic path; the reasoning uses an inverted action-result chain.

original_reasoning: Arabic describes a large fleshy woman, which aligns clearly with the Greek idea of thick-skinned or thick-bodied form.
semantic_journey: Arabic خضرف (khadraf) = a large, fleshy, ample woman; something thick and abundant. Greek παχύδερμος = thick-skinned (παχύς 'thick' + δέρμα 'skin'); also used for large thick-bodied creatures. Both encode the concept of large, thick-bodied, fleshy form. Arabic large-fleshy-body → Greek thick-skinned/thick-bodied is a direct 1-step parallel in the semantic field of largeness and bodily thickness.
key_insight: Arabic large-fleshy form (khadraf) and Greek thick-skinned/thick-bodied (pachydermos) share the concept of bodily thickness and ampleness.

original_reasoning: Arabic eating and drinking strongly fit a bowl or basin as the vessel that receives drink or food.
semantic_journey: Arabic سحب (sahaba) = to drag, pull, draw along; to drink by drawing (water). Greek σκάφη = a vessel, boat, basin, bowl (hollowed-out container). The reasoning: Arabic drinking → vessel for drinking. Arabic سحب means dragging/drawing, not a vessel. Even the 'drawing water' sense leads to: drawing-water → water-container — the Arabic names the action, not the vessel. Greek σκάφη is from σκάπτω (to hollow out) with its own etymology. No valid path.
key_insight: Arabic dragging/drawing-water and Greek hollow-vessel are different categories; Greek skaphe derives from 'hollowing out' not drawing.

original_reasoning: Mashaj - to mix between two vs 'to mix' - direct match on mixing
semantic_journey: Arabic مشج (mashaja) = to mix between two things, intermingle two materials. Greek μίσγω = to mix, mingle, blend together. Both encode the same direct mixing/mingling action. Arabic mixing-between-two → Greek mixing/mingling is a direct 1-step match — essentially the same word meaning the same thing. This is a confirmed cognate with high phonetic and semantic correspondence.
key_insight: Arabic mixing/mingling (mashaj) and Greek mixing/mingling (misgein) are direct cognates preserving the same action of blending two things.

original_reasoning: Arabic 'hairy' and Greek 'thistle' meet through a rough spiny-hairy surface concept.
semantic_journey: Arabic القنعاث (al-qanaath) = hairy, covered in rough hair. Greek λευκάκανθα = white thistle (λευκός 'white' + ἄκανθα 'thorn'). The reasoning: hairy → thorny/spiny. While hair and thorns are both surface projections, hairy (soft, flexible) and thorny (hard, rigid, white-colored) are distinct enough that no clean 1-2 step path exists. Furthermore, Greek λευκάκανθα has transparent internal etymology (white + thorn). No path.
key_insight: Arabic hairy and Greek white-thistled share only the loose category of surface projections; the Greek compound has its own transparent etymology.

original_reasoning: Arabic qirdim = 'pomegranate peel used for tanning; one who grabs'; Greek = 'garden cress/cardamom'. Clear Mediterranean loanword connection (cardamom was traded across Arabia).
semantic_journey: Arabic قرضم (qardim) = a plant related to the cardamom family; a biting/astringent plant used as a spice. Greek κάρδαμον = cress, cardamom (the pungent spice). The root q-r-d-m encodes the biting/pungent property that defines the spice. Both words name the same class of pungent spice plants. This is a material culture trade-route cognate — the pungent spice traveled with its Semitic name (as also seen in GRC_0165).
key_insight: Arabic qardim and Greek kardamon both name pungent spice plants in the cardamom/cress family — the Semitic root for this pungent spice preserved in Greek.

original_reasoning: Arabic soft earth receiving the locust's tail preserves a clear notion of insertion and being fixed in place, close to holding.
semantic_journey: Arabic سخخ (sakhkha) = soft earth into which something is inserted; the locust's tail inserting into ground. Greek ἐσχήκει = he had held/possessed (perfect of ἔχω). The connection: soft-earth-receiving-insertion → being fixed → holding. This is 3+ steps: receiving insertion → being embedded → fixed → holding. The same ἐσχήκει has appeared multiple times; each Arabic variant proposed is unconvincing. No path.
key_insight: Arabic soft-earth-insertion and Greek having/holding require too many intermediate steps; ἐσχήκει has been repeatedly mismatched in this dataset.

original_reasoning: blsm variant of balsam; still semitic root. Strong link.
semantic_journey: Arabic بلصم / بلسم (balsam) = balsam resin (same root as GRC_0207, variant form). Greek βάλσαμον = balsam (the aromatic resin tree and its product). This is the same confirmed Semitic-origin material culture term as GRC_0207 — the balsam tree and its aromatic resin traveled from the Near East into Greek with the Semitic name b-l-s-m intact.
key_insight: Arabic balsam (bilsam) is the Semitic origin of Greek balsamon — the same confirmed trade-route cognate as GRC_0207.

original_reasoning: Arabic explicitly means place, location, or valley-side, which aligns well with a named mountain landmark.
semantic_journey: Arabic الكمع (al-kama) = side, flank, place beside. Greek Λάκμων = Lacmon, a mountain in Epirus (proper toponym). The reasoning connects Arabic 'place/side' to a Greek mountain name. Λάκμων is a proper toponym with its own Greek or local Epirote etymology. Excluded: proper toponym.
key_insight: Λάκμων is a proper mountain toponym in Epirus; the Arabic flank/place connection is irrelevant to this proper name.

original_reasoning: Arabic خشم nose/snout and Greek spear-points share the concrete notion of projecting front tips.
semantic_journey: Arabic خشم (khashm) = nose, snout; the forward-projecting tip of the face. Greek αἰχμάς = spear-points, the forward-projecting tips of weapons. Both encode the concept of a forward-projecting pointed tip. Arabic nose/snout-tip (the forward projection of the face) → Greek spear-tip (the forward projection of a weapon) is a direct 1-step parallel: both are the prominent forward point of their respective objects.
key_insight: Arabic nose/snout-tip (khashm, forward projection of face) maps to Greek spear-tip (aikhme) — both are the foremost projecting point.

original_reasoning: Arabic 'intensify/be severe' aligns well with Greek 'terror' as intensified fear.
semantic_journey: Arabic دمس (damasa) = to intensify, be severe, make something dark/gloomy. Greek Δεῖμος = Deimos, personification of dread/terror (proper name, son of Ares). Both involve darkness/severity, but Δεῖμος is a proper divine name in Greek mythology. Even if the conceptual overlap were valid (intensity → dread), this is a proper name and excluded. Additionally, δεῖμα (dread) in Greek has its own etymology from δείδω (to fear).
key_insight: Δεῖμος is a proper divine name (Greek personification of terror) with its own Greek etymology; excluded as proper name.

original_reasoning: خثي refers to dunging or excrement, a direct material-to-ground relation with χθονί 'earth/ground'.
semantic_journey: Arabic خثي (khathy) = dunging, depositing excrement (animals dunging the ground). Greek χθονί = of the earth (dative of χθών). The reasoning: dung → earth (dung fertilizes earth). While ancient peoples understood dung-earth connection, Arabic names the act of dunging and Greek names the earth itself. The path requires: dunging → what is dunged → the earth — 3 steps. Also χθών is a deep Greek word with its own etymology; comparing it to Arabic dunging is too speculative.
key_insight: Arabic dunging and Greek earth are connected only through the agricultural use of dung as fertilizer — too indirect for a cognate.

original_reasoning: Claws or beast weapons directly rend apart, matching asunder on the result side.
semantic_journey: Arabic دخض (dakhada) = to push away; claws of a beast used to rend. Greek ἄνδιχα = asunder, apart, in two (adverb meaning split/separated). The reasoning: rending claws → being-asunder. While claws rend things apart, Arabic دخض means the weapon/instrument (claw), while Greek ἄνδιχα is the result state (apart). The chain instrument-of-tearing → tearing → being-torn → apart is 3+ steps. Too speculative.
key_insight: Arabic beast-claws/rending and Greek asunder/apart are action vs. result with too many intermediate steps.

original_reasoning: The Arabic gloss centers on a measure and measuring vessel, while καλκουλάτωρ is an accountant; both belong to reckoning and quantified handling.
semantic_journey: Arabic الكلج (al-kalj) = a measuring vessel or measure. Greek καλκουλάτωρ = accountant, calculator (from Latin calculātor). This is primarily a Latin loanword into Greek — καλκουλάτωρ derives from Latin calculus (pebble used in counting) + -tor. Arabic measuring vessel and Latin-derived Greek accountant share only the broad category of quantification/reckoning. Shared Latin loanword into Greek; no Arabic-to-Greek path.
key_insight: Greek kalkulatōr is a Latin loanword (from calculus); Arabic measuring vessel and Latin-Greek accountant share only generic quantification.

original_reasoning: ناموس=law/divine order; νεμεσήσεται=he will be indignant (divine retribution). Strong semantic link
semantic_journey: Arabic ناموس (namus) = divine law, sacred order, established code; also: the Law (used for Mosaic Law). Greek νεμεσήσεται = he will be divinely indignant (future of νεμεσάω, from Νέμεσις 'divine retribution'). Both encode divine law and its enforcement. Arabic divine-law → Greek divine-retribution-against-law-violation is a clear 1-step semantic shift: ناموس is the law, Nemesis is what happens when the law is violated. This is the law-enforcement relationship.
key_insight: Arabic divine law (namus) and Greek divine retribution (Nemesis) are two sides of the same concept — the law and the punishment for violating it.

original_reasoning: epiboētheō=help/send aid; b3th=send/wake. Strong semantic and phonetic match.
semantic_journey: Arabic بعثه (bathahu) = he sent him, dispatched him (same root as GRC_0217). Greek ἐπιβοηθέω = to send aid, come to assistance. Same assessment as GRC_0217: Arabic sending/dispatching → Greek sending-aid is a direct 1-step match. This is a duplicate of GRC_0217 with a slightly different Arabic form but the same root بعث.
key_insight: Same confirmed cognate as GRC_0217: Arabic sending/dispatching (bath) and Greek sending-aid (epiboetheo) share the core dispatch concept.

original_reasoning: Arabic faz' = 'terror, panic, fleeing for refuge'; Greek phyza = 'panicked rout'. Clear semantic match of panic/terror-induced flight.
semantic_journey: Arabic فزع (faza) = terror, panic; also the response to terror — fleeing for safety/refuge; to be in panic-flight. Greek φύζα = panicked rout, flight driven by terror (especially from battle). Both encode the concept of terror-induced panicked flight. Arabic terror/panic-flight → Greek terror-rout is a direct 1-step match: both describe the same phenomenon of panic causing collective flight.
key_insight: Arabic terror-panic-flight (faza) and Greek terror-rout (phyza) are direct cognates preserving the same concept of panic-driven flight.

original_reasoning: الجدي=young goat (also constellation Capricorn). αἰγίς=goat-skin (aegis). Both derive from the goat concept. الجدي IS a young goat, and αἰγίς is literally goat-hide. This is a strong masadiq match in the goat/caprine domain.
semantic_journey: Arabic الجدي (al-jady) = young goat, kid. Greek αἰγίδα = the aegis (accusative of αἰγίς = goat-skin shield). The root encodes young-goat, and the Greek word literally means 'goat-skin' — the protective covering made from a goat's hide. Both come from the goat concept: Arabic names the animal, Greek names the iconic object made from its skin. This is a direct material-culture preservation of the goat concept in the animal vs. its hide artifact.
key_insight: Arabic young-goat (jady) and Greek goat-skin shield (aigis) share the Semitic goat concept — the animal name preserved in the object made from its hide.

original_reasoning: Arabic root includes obscene or disordered speech, and Greek means spoken/said; both meet on the core of uttered speech.
semantic_journey: Arabic الخطل (al-khatal) = disordered/abusive speech. Greek λεχθείς = having been said (aorist passive participle of λέγω). Same assessment as GRC_0167: Arabic disordered speech vs. neutral Greek speaking participle. The Greek word is a generic past-spoken form with no connotation of disorder, abuse, or disordering. No valid semantic path.
key_insight: Arabic disordered/abusive speech and neutral Greek speaking participle do not share a valid semantic path.

original_reasoning: Greek 'aim/shoot at' matches the action side that yields the Arabic slain victim/result.
semantic_journey: Arabic سطح (sath) = flat surface; also a victim slaughtered and laid flat. Greek στοχάζομαι = to aim at a target, to guess/conjecture (from στόχος 'target mark'). The connection: Arabic slaughtered-flat → Greek well-aimed strike. The Arabic word primarily means flat surface; its possible slaughter sense is secondary. Greek στοχάζομαι has its own etymology from στόχος (a stake used as an archery target). No Semitic origin needed for this Greek word.
key_insight: Greek stokhazomai derives from stokhos (target stake) with no Semitic origin; Arabic flat-surface/slaughter is a different semantic field.

original_reasoning: هرقل=Heraclius (Byzantine emperor); Ἡρακλέους=Heracles. Direct Greek name loan via Roman emperor
semantic_journey: Arabic هرقل (Hirqil) = Heraclius, the Byzantine emperor (Arabic transliteration of the Greek name). Greek Ἡρακλέους = of Heracles (genitive of the mythological hero name). Both are proper names — Heraclius is the Roman emperor named after Heracles. Arabic هرقل is a loanword FROM Greek. This is a proper name borrowed from Greek into Arabic, not an Arabic-to-Greek cognate. Excluded.
key_insight: Arabic Hirqil is a borrowing of the Greek proper name Herakles/Herakleios; this is Greek→Arabic, not Arabic→Greek.

original_reasoning: Arabic warmth/heat can naturally yield softening; Greek 'soften, knead' fits that action-result chain.
semantic_journey: Arabic دفا (dafa) = warmth, gentle heat; to be warm. Greek δέφω = to soften by warmth, to knead with heat, to work material by warming. Same assessment as GRC_0198 (dafi variant). Arabic warmth → Greek soften-by-warmth is a direct 1-step match: the warmth acts to soften. This appears to be a duplicate of GRC_0198 with slightly different Arabic form (dafa vs. dafi).
key_insight: Arabic warmth (dafa) and Greek soften-by-warmth (depho) share the same confirmed path seen in GRC_0198.

original_reasoning: Arabic is to twist tightly; Greek is to take prisoner. Binding and captivity form a clear action-to-result chain.
semantic_journey: Arabic حملج (hamlaja) = to twist tightly, to spin tightly; to wind tightly around. Greek αἰχμαλόω = to take prisoner, to capture in war (from αἰχμάλωτος = 'taken with the spear'). The reasoning: tight twisting → binding → captivity. The chain twist → bind someone → take prisoner is a clear 2-step path: tightly winding rope around someone (binding them) is the ancient method of taking prisoners.
key_insight: Arabic tight-twisting/winding (hamlaja) maps to Greek taking-prisoner (aikhmaloo) through binding — twisting rope around captives.

original_reasoning: Sniffing/dry vs 'arid stalk, dry twig' - direct dryness match, strong link
semantic_journey: Arabic كرف (karrafa) = to sniff, smell; also: to become dry and shriveled (from dryness). Greek κάρφος = a dry twig, withered stalk, dry sliver of wood or straw. Both encode dryness/desiccation. Arabic drying/shriveling → Greek dry twig/stalk is a direct 1-step match: both describe the state of desiccated plant material.
key_insight: Arabic dryness/shriveling (karf) and Greek dry twig/stalk (karphos) share the concept of desiccated plant material.

original_reasoning: Arabic gives zeal, ardor, and bravery; Greek is spear points. Weapon and martial valor form a clear war-domain chain.
semantic_journey: Arabic حمس (hamas) = ardor, zeal, bravery, intense passionate commitment. Greek αἰχμάς = spear-points. The reasoning: martial ardor → spear weapon. While warriors with ardor carry spears, the connection is: Arabic bravery/zeal → warrior → weapon of warrior → spear-point — that is 3+ steps. The Greek word simply names the weapon; the Arabic names the emotional/motivational state. These are in different semantic categories.
key_insight: Arabic martial ardor/zeal and Greek spear-points are in different semantic categories — emotional state vs. physical weapon.

original_reasoning: Arabic gloss is a wild tree/plant and Greek is wild leek; the shared botanical-wild domain is a clear semantic chain.
semantic_journey: Arabic القرضي (al-qardiy) = a wild tree or shrub. Greek λυκόσκορδον = wild leek (λύκος 'wolf/wild' + σκόρδον 'garlic/leek'). Both are wild plants, but different plants in different families. Plant-category matching is insufficient. Greek λυκόσκορδον is a transparent compound (wolf-garlic = wild garlic/leek). No Semitic path to this Greek compound word.
key_insight: Arabic wild shrub and Greek wolf-garlic (wild leek) share only the botanical-wild category; Greek compound has transparent internal etymology.

original_reasoning: دفع and a kind of intercourse both preserve exchange or mutual taking, close to receive from another.
semantic_journey: Arabic دخم (dakhama) = to push, thrust; also: a kind of intercourse. Greek διαδέχομαι = to receive in succession, succeed to (see GRC_0176). The reasoning: mutual-thrusting → exchange → succession. Arabic mutual pushing is physical force, while Greek succession is abstract legal replacement. Same rejection as GRC_0176: 3+ steps between physical push and abstract succession.
key_insight: Arabic physical thrusting and Greek legal succession are too many conceptual steps apart.

original_reasoning: Arabic includes pot-foam whitening on boiling, which gives a real conceptual bridge to Greek paint in white.
semantic_journey: Arabic الكرفي (al-karfiy) = foam rising on boiling liquid; the white froth on a pot. Greek λευκογραφέω = to paint in white, to write in white. The reasoning: white pot-foam → painting white. Arabic foam is a temporary liquid phenomenon; Greek painting-white is a deliberate pigment application. The path: boiling foam → white color → applying white pigment — that is 3 steps, and the connection between ephemeral foam and deliberate painting is too weak.
key_insight: Arabic boiling foam and Greek white-painting share only the color white; the actions (accidental foam vs. deliberate painting) are unrelated.

original_reasoning: سقت 'without blessing/lacking' stands against the imperative 'have, hold'; this is a clear negation pair.
semantic_journey: Arabic سقت (saqata) = without blessing, devoid of (lacking), fallen/dropped. Greek σχέτε = hold! have! (aorist imperative of ἔχω). The reasoning: lacking → negation of having → 'have' as negation cognate. While lacking and having are opposites, a true negation cognate requires the Arabic root to encode BOTH sides of a concept. Arabic سقت means 'fell/dropped' or 'devoid of' — it is a deficiency word, not a bilateral concept. The negation-cognate claim is too weak here.
key_insight: Arabic deficiency/lacking and Greek hold/have are opposites but not a valid negation-cognate — the Arabic root does not encode both sides of the concept.

original_reasoning: Arabic الثنط: splitting/cracking (in a hadith: 'when the earth was spread out, it split'). Greek Θάνατος: Death. Death as the great splitting/tearing — the Arabic root means the very act that cosmologically creates boundaries, death being the ultimate splitting. Phonological match th-n-t vs. th-n-t.
semantic_journey: Arabic الثنط (al-thant) = splitting, cracking open of the earth. Greek Θάνατος = Death (personification of death in Greek mythology). The reasoning: cosmic splitting/cracking → death as the great splitting. While poetic, this is a 3+ step metaphorical overreach: earth-splitting → destruction → death → divine personification of death. Θάνατος has its own Greek etymology from θνῄσκω (to die). Excluded: Θάνατος is a proper divine name.
key_insight: Θάνατος is a proper Greek divine name (from thnesko 'to die') with transparent Greek etymology; Arabic earth-splitting is an unrelated concept.

original_reasoning: Arabic الدمانس = a place in Egypt and a village in Tiflis. Greek Δαμιανός = male name Damianos. The Egyptian place-name الدمانس is clearly a toponym derived from the Greek personal name Δαμιανός (Damianus), indicating direct phonological borrowing. This is a toponymic cognate.
semantic_journey: Arabic الدمانس (al-Damanis) = a place name in Egypt derived from the Greek personal name. Greek Δαμιανός = the proper name Damianos. The original reasoning acknowledges that the Arabic place name is itself derived FROM the Greek personal name. This is Greek → Arabic (the name entered Arabic as a toponym). Excluded: proper personal/place name, and the direction is inverted.
key_insight: Arabic al-Damanis is a toponym derived FROM Greek Damianos — the direction is Greek→Arabic, not Arabic→Greek.

original_reasoning: Arabic القردمانى = caraway (كروياء), described as a Roman/Levantine plant. Greek κάρδαμον = garden cress (Lepidium sativum), a pungent seed condiment. Both are pungent edible seeds used as food condiments/spices in the Mediterranean; the Arabic القردمانى specifically refers to the Greek/Roman κάρδαμον. Strong botanical-culinary parallel.
semantic_journey: Arabic القردم / القردمانى (al-qardamani) = caraway or a pungent seed condiment used in Roman/Levantine cooking. Greek κάρδαμον = garden cress (Lepidium sativum), a pungent-seeded condiment. Both name pungent seed condiments used in Mediterranean cooking. The root q-r-d-m encodes the biting/pungent property of these seeds. This is a material-culture trade-route cognate — the same family as GRC_0165 and GRC_0231. The Semitic name for this class of pungent condiment seeds entered Greek as kardamon.
key_insight: Arabic qardamani (caraway/pungent spice) and Greek kardamon (cress/pungent condiment) are the same Semitic spice-trade word family.

original_reasoning: Arabic القلفع = dried cracked mud that flakes off; iron scales from smithing; rough camel-skin flakes. Greek κέλυφος = outer shell/sheath/husk. Both involve a flaking/peeling outer layer — the Arabic القلفع = something that separates/flakes as an outer layer, matching κέλυφος = outer covering/shell that can be peeled.
semantic_journey: Arabic القلفع (al-qalfaa) = dried cracked mud that flakes off; iron scale/flakes from smithing; rough flaking surface layer. Greek κέλυφος = outer shell, husk, case, peelable outer covering. Both encode the concept of a brittle outer layer that can separate from the surface beneath. Arabic flaking-outer-layer → Greek shell/husk/peelable-covering is a direct 1-step match: both describe the outermost detachable layer of something.
key_insight: Arabic flaking outer layer (qalfaa) and Greek outer shell/husk (kelyphos) are direct cognates — both name the detachable outer layer.

original_reasoning: Arabic: chronic illness, lingering disease — a state of suffering pain. Greek: painkilling, pain-destroying. Both share the semantic field of pain/disease from opposite sides (affliction vs remedy). Clear mafahim connection on pain.
semantic_journey: Arabic الدنف (al-danf) = chronic illness, lingering painful disease, protracted suffering. Greek ὀδυνήφατος = painkilling, that which kills pain (ὀδύνη 'pain' + φατός 'killed/destroyed'). Both are in the semantic field of pain/disease — Arabic names the chronic suffering state, Greek names what destroys that suffering. This is a negation-cognate: Arabic is the affliction, Greek is its relief/elimination. The two sides of the same pain concept.
key_insight: Arabic chronic illness/pain (danf) and Greek painkilling (odynephatos) are two sides of the same concept — affliction vs. its destruction.

original_reasoning: Arabic: الرزداق (Arabicized from Rustā = village, rural settlement / rows of palms). Greek: of or belonging to the root (rhiza). Both share the concept of rural/agrarian rootedness — village settlement vs root-level agricultural concept. Clear combined link.
semantic_journey: Arabic الرزداق (al-ruzdak) = Arabicized form of Persian rusta (village, rural settlement). Greek ῥιζικός = of the root (from ῥίζα 'root'). The Arabic form is a Persian loanword into Arabic (not an Arabic root at all). The Greek derives from ῥίζα (root) which is its own Greek word. These are unrelated words from different source languages that happen to sound similar. No Arabic-to-Greek path; the Arabic form itself is Persian-derived.
key_insight: Arabic ruzdak is a Persian loanword; Greek rhizikos derives from rhiza (root) — these are unrelated words from different sources.

original_reasoning: Arabic: spear/lance — the core weapon used in battle. Greek: 'rousing the fight' — battle-rousing epithet. Both are deeply in the semantic field of armed combat/warfare. The spear IS the instrument of battle-rousing in ancient warfare.
semantic_journey: Arabic الرمح (al-rumh) = spear, lance. Greek ἐγρεμάχης = battle-rousing (ἔγρω 'rouse/awaken' + μάχη 'battle'). The Greek is a transparent compound meaning 'one who rouses battle.' Arabic spear → Greek battle-rouser requires: spear → the person who wields it → the person who provokes fighting — that is 3+ steps, and the Greek compound's meaning derives entirely from its transparent Greek components. No Semitic path needed or supported.
key_insight: Arabic spear and Greek battle-rouser compound are too far apart; the Greek compound has transparent internal Greek etymology.

original_reasoning: Arabic: السمسار = broker/intermediary between buyer and seller. Greek: σαμψήρα = shamshir sword (a curved blade). Both are likely loanwords from Persian (simsār = broker; shamshir = curved sword). Clear cultural trade-mediation link — the broker who handles weapons and goods. Combined semantic field of commerce/mediation.
